parentQuestion: Hair cells receptive to changes in dynamic equilibrium are found in the crista ampullaris.

scaffolds.q: 'Dynamic' equilibrium refers to sensing ROTATIONAL movement (turning, spinning).
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Dynamic = moving. Sensing when your head rotates in space.

scaffolds.q: The semicircular canals detect rotational head movements.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Three canals oriented perpendicular to each other sense rotation in all directions.

scaffolds.q: There are THREE semicircular canals, each detecting rotation in a different plane.
Each semicircular canal has a swelling called the ampulla at its base.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! The ampulla contains the sensory apparatus for detecting rotation.

scaffolds.q: The crista ampullaris is a ridge of hair cells inside each ampulla.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The crista ('crest') has hair cells topped by the cupula.

scaffolds.q: The cupula is a gelatinous mass that bends when fluid (endolymph) moves.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Head rotation makes endolymph lag behind, pushing the cupula.

scaffolds.q: When you turn your head, endolymph movement bends the cupula and hair cells.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Fluid inertia deflects the cupula → hair cell bending → neural signal.

scaffolds.q: The crista ampullaris detects angular (rotational) acceleration of the head.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! When you start or stop turning, the cristae sense the rotation.

scaffolds.q: Hair cells in the crista ampullaris (of semicircular canals) detect dynamic equilibrium.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The cristae detect rotational head movement via endolymph flow deflecting the cupula.
